back: A cardinal number is a generalization of the natural numbers used to measure the cardinality (size) of a set.

front: Define the order relation ≤ between two cardinal numbers, |A| and |B|.
back: We say |A| ≤ |B| if there exists an injective function (one-to-one) from set A into set B.

front: What does the Cantor-Schröder-Bernstein theorem state regarding cardinal order?
back: If |A| ≤ |B| and |B| ≤ |A|, then |A| = |B|. This establishes that the order relation ≤ is antisymmetric.

front: How is the strict order relation < defined for cardinal numbers?
back: We say |A| < |B| if |A| ≤ |B| but it is not true that |B| ≤ |A| (i.e., there is an injection from A to B but no bijection between them).

front: What is the significance of the order relation in cardinal numbers?
back: It allows us to compare the sizes of infinite sets, forming a well-defined partial order (which the Cantor-Schröder-Bernstein theorem shows is antisymmetric) that is also a total order, a fact equivalent to the Axiom of Choice.
